supabase_cases.lead_counsel: Robbins Geller; Frank R. Cruz; Glancy Prongay
supabase_cases.case_summary: Filed 03/25/2026 N.D. Cal. DOJ unsealed indictment 03/19/2026 against 3 individuals for diverting $2.5B of AI servers to China (export control violations). Class period: 04/30/2024-03/19/2026. LP deadline: 05/26/2026.

According to the Complaint, Super Micro Computer, Inc. is an international company that develops, manufactures, and provides server and storage systems for various markets, including data centers, cloud computing, AI, 5G, and edge computing. This lawsuit was filed against Super Micro and two of its Officers.
During the Class Period, Defendants provided investors with material information concerning SMCI’s financial results for the fiscal years 2021 through 2024. Defendants’ statements included, among other things, reports of continued significant growth with increasing financial success year after year, a healthy relationship with its related parties, and was in compliance with United States export restrictions. The Complaint alleges that Defendants provided these overwhelmingly positive statements to investors while, at the same time, disseminating materially false and misleading statements and/or concealing material adverse facts concerning the true state of SMCI’s accounting; notably, that it was subject to consistent overreporting of sales and underreporting of expenses, that it had re-hired multiple executives who departed in the wake of the Company’s prior accounting scandal, that the Company has a closer relationship to its related parties than disclosed, that SMCI had more related parties than it had disclosed, and that the Company had not ceased exporting products to areas restricted by the United States government as a result of the Russia-Ukraine war, risking government sanction.
On August 27, 2024, Hindenburg Research unveiled a short report on SMCI. The short report detailed several allegations against the Company, including that Hindenburg “found glaring accounting red flags, evidence of undisclosed related party transactions, sanctions and control failures, and customer issues.” The Complaint alleges that investors and analysts reacted immediately to these revelations leading to a dramatic decline in the price of the Company's common stock.
On July 10, 2025, the Court issued an Order appointing Lead Plaintiff and Counsel. On July 14, the Court issued an Order consolidating cases. All future docketing was ordered to be done in the Lead Case 24-CV-06147.S

According to the law firm press release, Super Micro Computer, Inc. designs, develops, manufactures and sells server solutions based on modular and open-standard architecture. The Company's products include servers, motherboards, chassis, and accessories.
The Complaint alleges that throughout the Class Period, Defendants made materially false and misleading statements regarding the Company’s business, operations and compliance policies. Specifically, Defendants made false and/or misleading statements and/or failed to disclose that: (i) Super Micro’s financial statements contained accounting errors, including errors with respect to one of the Company’s sales transactions; (ii) as such, the Company’s internal controls were not effective; (iii) Super Micro lacked the capability to timely review and assess the impact of the foregoing issues; and (iv) as a result, Super Micro’s public statements were materially false and misleading at all relevant times.
On May 25, 2018, the Court issued an Order consolidating cases and appointing Lead Plaintiff and Counsel. All future docketing was ordered to be done in the lead case 18-CV-00838.
Lead Plaintiff filed a consolidated Complaint on September 24. On January 22, 2019, Lead Plaintiff filed an amended consolidated Complaint. Lead Plaintiff filed a second amended consolidated Complaint on June 21. Defendants filed a Motion to Dismiss the amended consolidated Complaint on July 26. On March 23, 2020, the Court issued an Order granting Defendants' Motion to Dismiss. Plaintiff was given leave to amend the Complaint.
Lead Plaintiff filed a third amended consolidated Complaint on April 22. Defendants filed a Motion to Dismiss the third amended consolidated Complaint on June 5. On October 14, Lead Plaintiff filed a fourth amended consolidated Complaint. Defendants re-noticed the Motion to Dismiss on October 28. On March 29, 2021, the Court issued an Order granting in part and denying in part Defendants' Motion to Dismiss. The Court denied leave to amend the Complaint.
On March 11, 2022, the parties notified the Court that they had reached an agreement in principle to settle the case. The parties entered into a Stipulation of Settlement on April 8. On November 8, the Court granted preliminary approval of the Settlement. On May 5, 2023, the Court granted final approval of the Settlement, including an award of Attorneys’ Fees and Expenses.

According to the law firm press release, Super Micro Computer, Inc. develops and provides high performance server solutions based on modular and open-standard architecture. The Company offers a range of server, storage, blade, workstation, and full rack solutions, as well as networking devices, server management software, and technology support and services. It also provides a range of application optimized server solutions, including rackmount and blade server systems; and server subsystems and accessories comprising server boards, and chassis and power supplies, as well as other system accessories, including microprocessors, and memory and disc drives. The Company offers its products to data center, cloud computing, enterprise IT, big data, high performance computing, and embedded markets.
The Complaint alleges that throughout the Class Period, Defendants made false and/or misleading statements, as well as failed to disclose material adverse facts about the Company’s business, operations, and prospects. Specifically, Defendants made false and/or misleading statements and/or failed to disclose that: (i) the Company improperly recorded expenses in its financial reports; (ii) as a result, the Company’s reported net income was misstated; (iii) the Company lacked adequate internal financial controls; and (iv) as a result of the above, the Company’s financial statements were materially false and misleading at all relevant times.
On August 31, 2015, post-market, Super Micro Computer announced that the Company “has determined that it is unable to file its Annual Report on Form 10-K for the fiscal year ended June 30, 2015 within the prescribed time period without unreasonable effort or expense. [Super Micro Computer] recently discovered certain irregularities regarding certain marketing expenses and additional time is required for [the Company] to complete its investigation of the matter.”
On this news, shares of Super Micro Computer declined $2.58 per share, or 9.43%, to close at $24.77 on September 1, 2015.
Plaintiffs filed a consolidated Complaint on April 1, 2016.
On September 29, 2017, the Court issued an Order granting Defendants' Motion to Dismiss, with leave to amend. Plaintiff filed an amended Complaint on December 1. On January 12, 2018, Defendants filed a Motion to Dismiss the amended Complaint. On June 27, the Court issued an Order granting Defendants' Motion to Dismiss and entered Judgment in favor of the Defendants. On July 24, Plaintiff filed a Notice appealing the Court's Order of Dismissal and Judgment. On November 1, Plaintiff voluntarily dismissed the appeal.

risk_card.data_coverage_note: SCA database contains ~6,982 filings (~35% of estimated universe). NERA/Cornerstone are authoritative full-universe references.

screening_questions.question: Has the company disclosed any material weakness or significant deficiency in internal controls in the last 3 years?
screening_questions.why_it_matters: Material weaknesses are the #1 precursor to restatements. Restatements shift avg settlement from $48M to $152M — a 3.2x multiplier.
screening_questions.upgrade_criteria: Clean SOX 404 opinions for 3+ consecutive years with no significant deficiencies
screening_questions.downgrade_criteria: Any material weakness disclosed, especially if unremediated. Significant deficiency in revenue recognition or financial close process.

screening_questions.question: Does the company have complex revenue recognition (ASC 606)? Multiple performance obligations, variable consideration, or long-term contracts?
screening_questions.why_it_matters: Revenue recognition complexity is the single most common allegation type in accounting fraud SCAs. ASC 606 implementation created new attack surfaces.
screening_questions.upgrade_criteria: Simple revenue model (product ships, revenue recognized). Single performance obligation per contract.
screening_questions.downgrade_criteria: Multiple element arrangements, bill-and-hold, percentage of completion, channel stuffing risk, significant variable consideration

screening_questions.question: Is this a repeat SCA target? How many prior filings, and what were the outcomes?
screening_questions.why_it_matters: 25% of all SCAs involve companies sued before. 46 companies have been sued 4+ times. Prior history is a base-rate multiplier — plaintiffs bar has institutional memory.
screening_questions.upgrade_criteria: No prior SCAs. Or: 1 prior SCA that was dismissed, >5 years ago.
screening_questions.downgrade_criteria: Any prior settled SCA. 2+ prior filings regardless of outcome. Active ongoing SCA.

screening_questions.question: CFO tenure: how long has the current CFO been in role? Any CFO changes in last 18 months?
screening_questions.why_it_matters: The MSA lesson: a 4-month CFO was missed in diligence. Short CFO tenure correlates with both (a) inherited accounting problems and (b) new CFO discovering and disclosing issues.
screening_questions.upgrade_criteria: CFO tenure >3 years, no recent changes, prior CFO departed for normal reasons
screening_questions.downgrade_criteria: CFO tenure <18 months, especially if predecessor departed abruptly or "to pursue other opportunities"

screening_questions.question: Has the stock experienced a >15% single-day drop in the last 12 months?
screening_questions.why_it_matters: Stock drops >15% are the mechanical trigger for plaintiffs firms to evaluate filing. Larger drops = larger damages = higher settlement.
screening_questions.upgrade_criteria: No single-day drop >10% in 12 months. Low volatility stock.
screening_questions.downgrade_criteria: Any single-day drop >15%. Multiple drops >10%. Stock down >30% from 12-month high.

screening_questions.question: What is the delta between GAAP and non-GAAP earnings? Is the non-GAAP adjustment >20% of GAAP?
screening_questions.why_it_matters: Large GAAP/non-GAAP deltas create the "earnings quality" allegations that dominate accounting fraud SCAs. Plaintiffs argue the non-GAAP figures are misleading.
screening_questions.upgrade_criteria: Non-GAAP adjustments <10% of GAAP, limited to standard items (stock comp, amortization)
screening_questions.downgrade_criteria: Non-GAAP adjustments >20% of GAAP, includes unusual addbacks, company leads investor communications with non-GAAP

screening_questions.question: Is the auditor a Big 4 firm? Any auditor changes in the last 24 months?
screening_questions.why_it_matters: Non-Big 4 auditors correlate with higher restatement risk. Auditor changes mid-cycle are a red flag for disagreements on accounting treatment.
screening_questions.upgrade_criteria: Big 4 auditor, same firm for 5+ years, clean opinions
screening_questions.downgrade_criteria: Non-Big 4 auditor, or auditor change within 24 months, especially if prior auditor resigned (vs. was replaced)

screening_questions.question: What is short interest as a % of float? Has it increased >50% in the last 90 days?
screening_questions.data_source: FINRA short interest data, short seller report databases
screening_questions.why_it_matters: Rising short interest is a leading indicator of both stock drops and short-seller reports that trigger SCAs (Hercules Capital/HTGC pattern).
screening_questions.upgrade_criteria: Short interest <3% of float, stable or declining.
screening_questions.downgrade_criteria: Short interest >8% of float, or any increase >50% in 90 days. Named in short seller report.

screening_questions.question: What is the market cap? How does it map to the settlement severity curve for the relevant scenario?
screening_questions.why_it_matters: Market cap is the strongest predictor of settlement size — its the damages denominator. A $50B company with a 20% drop has a $10B damages pool.
screening_questions.upgrade_criteria: Market cap <$1B with simple business model — severity likely <$10M even if case settles.
screening_questions.downgrade_criteria: Market cap >$10B — enters the zone where P90 settlements exceed $100M. Tower adequacy becomes critical.
